narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 51% chance that you would not pass. This prediction draws from patterns seen in thousands of past students, focusing on key indicators like grades, test scores, and enrollment status—without considering gender or race. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 grade decile, which ranked quite low at 1 compared to the average of about 6 for those who passed the bar, pushing the prediction firmly toward expecting failure. This was the single biggest factor tipping the scales against you, as lower performance in later years often signals challenges ahead in rigorous legal training. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which actually stands out positively—higher than the 3.24 average among bar passers—and pulled the prediction toward success, softening the overall risk assessment somewhat. Your LSAT score of 34 followed closely, also working in your favor by nudging the model toward expecting you to pass, though it fell short of the typical 37.27 seen in successful students. Other aspects, like your lower-middle family income bracket and part-time enrollment plans—which are less common among bar passers—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. Compared to admitted students, who often have stronger mid-to-late grade deciles and full-time commitments, your profile showed promise in undergrad work but raised concerns from that Year 3 dip. While this outcome is disappointing, especially with your solid GPA foundation, it highlights where the model saw the greatest hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to passing averages, strengthening later-year grades through targeted prep if possible, or exploring programs that support part-time students with similar backgrounds. You might also appeal with evidence of recent improvements or seek schools using holistic reviews beyond bar predictions. With focused steps on those grade trends, you can reshape your path to success.
